Name: spectral_latency_aware_inference
Title: Spectral Latency-Aware Inference: A Training-Free Framework for Efficient and Robust Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds and Jacobian-based layer-skipping can be used to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference, enabling efficient and robust machine learning without requiring retraining or calibration.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in training-free precision allocation, geometric interference cancellation, and spectral quantization routing. However, our approach differs in its focus on spectral stability bounds and Jacobian-based layer-skipping, which enables a more efficient and robust inference framework. Unlike existing methods, our proposal does not rely on static profiling, costly calibration, or heuristic control mechanisms, making it more suitable for real-world deployment.
Abstract: We propose a novel training-free framework for efficient and robust machine learning inference, leveraging spectral stability bounds and Jacobian-based layer-skipping to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference. Our approach, Spectral Latency-Aware Inference (SLAI), dynamically allocates numerical precision and skips layers based on real-time activation geometry, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground SLAI in information-theoretic bounds, showing that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement. Empirically, we demonstrate that SLAI matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead by over 70% and enforcing deterministic latency bounds.
Experiments: Evaluate SLAI on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, comparing its performance to full-space perturbation baselines and existing training-free methods.

Assess the impact of spectral stability bounds and Jacobian-based layer-skipping on inference efficiency and robustness, using metrics such as throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers and stability radii.
Investigate the applicability of SLAI to various machine learning models and tasks, including natural language processing and computer vision.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may not be suitable for tasks requiring high numerical precision or complex optimization landscapes.
The effectiveness of SLAI may depend on the quality of the spectral stability bounds and Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanisms, which may require careful tuning and calibration.
